pages.title: Deployment Runbook
pages.body:
How to deploy AcmeTech services to production.

STEPS:
1. Merge your PR to main. CI builds the release artifact automatically.
2. Deploy the artifact to staging with `make deploy ENV=staging`.
3. Manually verify your change on staging (click through the affected flows).
4. Deploy straight to production with `make deploy ENV=prod`.
5. Watch the error dashboard for 10 minutes after the deploy.

ROLLBACK:
If something looks wrong, redeploy the previous artifact with `make deploy ENV=prod VERSION=<previous>`. Ask Elena or Marcus before rolling back — rollbacks touch shared infrastructure.

NOTES:
- Deploys are allowed any time before 4pm. No Friday deploys.
- Database migrations run automatically as part of the deploy.
- If staging looks broken right after a deploy, wait a couple of minutes and retry.

Contact: Elena Petrov (DevOps) for pipeline issues.
pages.author: Elena Petrov

pages.title: Incident Response Playbook
pages.body:
What to do when production breaks.

SEVERITY LEVELS:
- P0: full outage or data loss. Page the on-call immediately for any P0.
- P1: major degradation with a workaround available.
- P2: partial or minor impact.
When in doubt, page anyway — false pages are free.

RESPONSE PROCESS:
1. Whoever detects the incident declares it in #incidents with severity.
2. First responder opens the war room in Slack and owns the timeline.
3. Page the on-call for P0s; P1s go to the service owner first.
4. Assign someone to customer comms — status page within 15 minutes, enterprise notifications per the SLA process.
5. Mitigate first, investigate after. Rollback is always an acceptable mitigation.
6. Declare recovery in the war room with a timestamp.

POSTMORTEMS:
- Postmortem must be filed within 48 hours by the first responder.
- Format: timeline, root cause, customer impact, what went well, action items with owners.
- Blameless: we postmortem the system, not the person.
- Action items get the `postmortem` label in Jira and are prioritized above feature work.
- Thursday standup reviews all open postmortems.

P1s with small blast radius may use the lightweight format: one pager, no meeting.
pages.author: Raj Patel

pages.title: Refund Policy
pages.body:
Official refund policy for AcmeTech subscriptions.

REFUND WINDOW:
Customers may request a full refund within 15 days of the charge date. Requests after 15 days are denied — point the customer to the terms of service. The 15-day window applies to monthly plans only; annual plans are non-refundable after activation.

PROCESS:
1. Verify the charge date in the billing panel.
2. If within 15 days, process the refund through Stripe directly.
3. Note the refund on the customer account with the reason.

ESCALATION:
Refunds over $1,000 require support-lead sign-off. For enterprise accounts, contact the account manager.

EXCEPTIONS:
No exceptions to the 15-day window without VP approval.
pages.author: Tom O'Connell

pages.title: New Hire Onboarding Checklist
pages.body:
Checklist for onboarding new engineers at AcmeTech.

BEFORE DAY 1 (manager):
- Order laptop and peripherals
- Create email account
- Request VPN access via the IT portal: https://vpn.acmetech.internal/request
- Add to the engineering mailing list

DAY 1:
- HR paperwork and benefits enrollment
- Install the VPN client and verify connectivity
- Clone the main repositories
- Set up the local development environment (see the Dev Environment page — allow a full day)
- Intro meetings with the team

WEEK 1:
- Complete security training
- First ticket: pick something from the `good-first-issue` label
- Meet your onboarding buddy

ACCESS:
- Staging access is granted on day 1.
- Production access requires manager approval and the security training certificate.

Questions? Ask in #onboarding.
pages.author: Marcus Webb

pages.title: Code Review Guidelines
pages.body:
How we review code at AcmeTech.

APPROVALS:
- Anything that ships to production requires 2 approvals before merge.
- Hotfixes may merge with 1 approval, but a follow-up review ticket must be filed within 24 hours. Mark hotfixes with the `hotfix` label AND a [HOTFIX] title prefix.
- Docs-only and test-only changes need 1 approval.

REVIEWER EXPECTATIONS:
- Actually read the diff. For changes over 100 lines, run the code or trace the logic — a 45-second LGTM is not a review.
- First review pass within 24 hours (team SLA).
- Comment on the approach before nitpicking style; the linter owns style.

AUTHOR EXPECTATIONS:
- Keep PRs under ~400 lines where possible; split otherwise.
- Never force-push over review comments — it destroys thread context. Append commits, squash at merge.
- Respond to every comment, even if just with a resolution emoji.

APPENDIX — DATABASE MIGRATIONS:
- Two-release rule: never drop a column in the same release that stops using it. Release N stops reading, release N+1 drops. This preserves rollback safety.
- Always run migrations against the staging snapshot first: `make migrate-staging`.
- Migrations that lock large tables need a review from the DB owner.
pages.author: Marcus Webb

pages.title: API Rate Limiting Policy
pages.body:
How rate limiting works on the AcmeTech API.

MODEL:
- One global token bucket per API key (not per endpoint).
- Current plans: 600 requests/minute sustained, burst to 1,200.
- Legacy plans (pre-2025 signups): 300 requests/minute, burst to 600. Legacy accounts are being migrated — check the plan code in the admin panel before debugging a throttling complaint.

BEHAVIOR:
- Throttled requests receive HTTP 429 with a Retry-After header indicating seconds until the bucket refills. Clients should honor it.
- Rate limit headers on every response: X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset.

ENTERPRISE:
- Enterprise plans have custom limits set per contract; the limiter service is the source of truth, not the plan table.

SUPPORT NOTES:
- 'Throttled at half the documented limit' almost always means a legacy plan.
- Customers cannot currently see their own usage; a usage dashboard is planned (ACME-254).
pages.author: Priya Sharma

pages.title: On-Call Rotation
pages.body:
How on-call works at AcmeTech.

ROTATION:
- Weekly rotations, Monday 10:00 UTC handoff.
- Primary + secondary. Secondary is paged if primary doesn't ack within 5 minutes.
- Rotation includes: Elena, Raj, Priya, Lucy, Marcus. New joiners shadow a full rotation before joining — you page WITH someone before you page alone.

HANDOFF:
- Outgoing primary posts handoff notes in the on-call doc: open alerts, ongoing weirdness, tuning changes.

PAGING POLICY:
- P0: page immediately, always.
- P1: page during business hours; after hours, page if customer-facing.
- False pages are free — when in doubt, page.
- Alert fatigue is a bug: if an alert pages twice without action needed, tune or delete it.

COMPENSATION:
- On-call week carries a stipend; incident hours after midnight convert to time off in lieu.

ESCALATION CHAIN:
Primary → Secondary → Engineering Lead (Marcus) → CTO (Sarah).
pages.author: Raj Patel

pages.title: Sprint Planning Process
pages.body:
How AcmeTech plans sprints.

CADENCE:
- Two-week sprints. Planning Thursday 10:00 for the sprint starting Monday.

PROCESS:
1. PMs bring ranked candidates with PRDs that have already passed tech review — engineering finding out about a feature at sprint planning is too late.
2. Engineering brings capacity (adjusted for rotations, on-call, ramping new hires).
3. The team commits together. Nothing enters the sprint after commit without swapping something of equal size out.
4. Escalations for mid-sprint additions go through the PM, never directly to engineers.

CARRY-OVERS:
- Carry-over work keeps its original estimate minus progress — don't re-estimate nearly-done work as fresh.

PRIORITIZATION PRINCIPLES:
- Postmortem action items outrank feature work, by policy.
- We are a dev tools company: the API is the product. Dashboard polish never outranks API reliability.

RETRO:
- Last Friday of the sprint. Velocity, carry-overs, and one process experiment per sprint, max.
pages.author: Dan Kim

pages.title: Customer Escalation Matrix
pages.body:
When and how support escalates to engineering and accounts.

IMMEDIATE ESCALATION (page if outside hours):
- Data loss or suspected data loss
- Security issues (auth bypass, data exposure, suspicious access)
- Billing errors (wrong charges, failed refunds, duplicate charges)
Post in #incidents with: ticket link + customer impact + what you verified. Say what you tested, not just 'X is broken'. Include the request id from the customer's error page if available.

STANDARD ESCALATION:
- Everything else: file a Jira bug, engineering triages at next standup.

SLA (ENTERPRISE):
- P1: 1 hour first response. P2: 4 hours. Clock starts when the ticket lands, does not pause overnight for enterprise.
- Breach = service credits: 5% of monthly fee per breach, capped at 20% per month.
- 2 breaches in a quarter triggers an exec review with the account — page the account manager BEFORE a breach, not after.
- If a P1 is 40 minutes old and untouched, escalate to the support lead and account manager immediately.

REFUND THRESHOLDS:
- Self-serve: under $1,000 process directly; $1,000–5,000 support-lead sign-off; over $5,000 goes to finance.
- Enterprise: any refund over $5,000 — loop in the account manager before processing.

CONTACTS:
Support lead: Tom O'Connell. Account manager (enterprise): Emma Wright. Engineering escalation: on-call via #incidents.
pages.author: Tom O'Connell

pages.title: Architecture Overview
pages.body:
High-level architecture of the AcmeTech platform.

CORE SERVICES:
- api-gateway: routing, authentication, rate limiting. Config-driven; config changes deploy through the same canary pipeline as code.
- auth-service: sessions, API keys, SSO (SAML/OIDC). Validates JWTs against the IdP's JWKS.
- billing-service: subscriptions, invoicing, Stripe integration. All webhook handling must be idempotent — provider retries are routine.
- webhook-dispatcher: outbound event delivery with exponential backoff (1/4/16/64s, 5 attempts, then parked for manual retry).
- search-indexer: Kafka consumer maintaining the search index; lag alert at 15 minutes.
- checkout-service: order flow; hot path is the orders-by-customer lookup, index-dependent.

DATA:
- Postgres (primary + read replica, failover target 90s), Redis (sessions + caches, all keys require explicit TTL), S3 (uploads and exports, hash-sharded key prefixes), Kafka (event backbone).

DEPLOYMENT:
- Kubernetes. Deploys go staging → smoke tests → 5% canary → auto-promote on clean metrics.
- Observability: Grafana dashboards, alerting on error rate, p99 latency, DB pool saturation (80%), Redis memory (80%), search lag.

PRINCIPLES:
- The API is the product — reliability outranks features.
- Rollback first, investigate after.
- Everything that pages a human must have a runbook link in the alert.
pages.author: Sarah Chen
